content.problemStatement:
There is a line consisting of NNN piles of coins on the table. For each iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N), the iii-th pile contains CiC_iCi​ coins, each with value ViV_iVi​. There is also a bin under the table.

Alice and Bob play a game, moving alternately. A move consists of two steps:


Throw a prefix of the piles in the bin (the prefix can be empty). Then, if there are no coins left on the table, the game ends. Note that in this step, it is not allowed to throw away only some coins in a pile — the entire pile must be thrown into the bin.
Take exactly one coin from the first (remaining) pile. Then, if there are no coins left on the table, the game ends.
Let AAA and BBB be the sum of the values of the coins taken by Alice and Bob, respectively.

The score of the game is A−BA - BA−B. Alice wants to maximize the score; Bob wants to minimize it.

Alice moves first. What's the score of the game if Alice and Bob play optimally?

Please look at the sample cases below for an example of how the game is played.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
Each test cases consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the number of piles of coins.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers C1,C2,…,CNC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_NC1​,C2​,…,CN​: CiC_iCi​ is the number of coins in the iii-th pile.
The third line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers V1,V2,…,VNV_1, V_2, \ldots, V_NV1​,V2​,…,VN​: ViV_iVi​ is the value of each coin in the iii-th pile.




Output Format
For each test case, print on a new line a single integer: the final value of A−BA - BA−B, if Alice and Bob play optimally.
constraints: It is guaranteed that the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: An optimal strategy for Alice and Bob is the following:

Initially, the piles, denoted by (Ci,Vi)(C_i, V_i)(Ci​,Vi​), are ((1,38),(2,46),(5,66),(1,64),(1,59),(3,69))((1, 38), (2, 46), (5, 66), (1, 64), (1, 59), (3, 69))((1,38),(2,46),(5,66),(1,64),(1,59),(3,69)).
Alice throws the first 555 piles in the bin. The piles are now ((3,69))((3, 69))((3,69)). Then, she takes a coin from the first pile. The piles are now ((2,69))((2, 69))((2,69)).
Bob doesn't throw any pile in the bin. Then, he takes a coin from the first pile. The piles are now ((1,69))((1, 69))((1,69)).
Alice doesn't throw any pile in the bin. Then, she takes a coin from the first pile. There are no piles left and the game ends.
A=69+69=138A = 69 + 69 = 138A=69+69=138, B=69B = 69B=69, A−B=69A - B = 69A−B=69.

Test case 222: An optimal strategy for Alice and Bob is the following:

Initially, the piles, denoted by (Ci,Vi)(C_i, V_i)(Ci​,Vi​), are ((2,49),(5,24),(4,17),(1,54),(1,23),(2,50))((2, 49), (5, 24), (4, 17), (1, 54), (1, 23), (2, 50))((2,49),(5,24),(4,17),(1,54),(1,23),(2,50)).
Alice throws the first 333 piles in the bin. The piles are now ((1,54),(1,23),(2,50))((1, 54), (1, 23), (2, 50))((1,54),(1,23),(2,50)). Then, she takes a coin from the first pile. The piles are now ((1,23),(2,50))((1, 23), (2, 50))((1,23),(2,50)).
Bob doesn't throw any pile in the bin. Then, he takes a coin from the first pile. The piles are now ((2,50))((2, 50))((2,50)).
Alice throws the first pile in the bin. There are no piles left and the game ends.
A=54A = 54A=54, B=23B = 23B=23, A−B=31A - B = 31A−B=31.
